content.text:
import { io, Socket } from 'socket.io-client'; type ConnectionStatus = 'connected' | 'connecting' | 'disconnected'; type EventHandler = (data: any) => void; class WebSocketClient { private socket: Socket | null = null; private url: string; private token: string | null = null; private status: ConnectionStatus = 'disconnected'; private reconnectAttempts: number = 0; private maxReconnectAttempts: number = 5; private reconnectDelays: number[] = [1000, 2000, 4000, 8000, 16000]; // Exponential backoff private messageQueue: Array<{ event: string; data: any }> = []; private eventHandlers: Map> = new Map(); private statusChangeHandlers: Set<(status: ConnectionStatus) => void> = new Set(); constructor(url: string = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_WS_URL || 'http://localhost:3001') { this.url = url; } connect(url?: string, token?: string): void { if (this.socket?.connected) { console.warn('WebSocket already connected'); return; } if (url) this.url = url; if (token) this.token = token; this.setStatus('connecting'); this.socket = io(this.url, { auth: { token: this.token, }, transports: ['websocket', 'polling'], reconnection: false, // We handle reconnection manually }); this.setupEventListeners(); } private setupEventListeners(): void { if (!this.socket) return; this.socket.on('connect', () => { console.log('WebSocket connected'); this.setStatus('connected'); this.reconnectAttempts = 0; this.flushMessageQueue(); }); this.socket.on('disconnect', (reason) => { console.log('WebSocket disconnected:', reason); this.setStatus('disconnected'); // Attempt reconnection if not manually disconnected if (reason !== 'io client disconnect') { this.attemptReconnect(); } }); this.socket.on('connect_error', (error) => { console.error('WebSocket connection error:', error); this.setStatus('disconnected'); this.attemptReconnect(); }); // Forward all events to registered handlers this.socket.onAny((event, data) => { this.triggerEventHandlers(event, data); }); } private attemptReconnect(): void { if (this.reconnectAttempts >= this.maxReconnectAttempts) { console.error('Max reconnection attempts reached'); return; } const delay = this.reconnectDelays[this.reconnectAttempts] || 16000; this.reconnectAttempts++; console.log(`Attempting reconnection ${this.reconnectAttempts}/${this.maxReconnectAttempts} in ${delay}ms`); setTimeout(() => { if (this.status === 'disconnected') { this.connect(); } }, delay); } disconnect(): void { if (this.socket) { this.socket.disconnect(); this.socket = null; } this.setStatus('disconnected'); this.reconnectAttempts = 0; this.messageQueue = []; } emit(event: string, data: any): void { if (this.socket?.connected) { this.socket.emit(event, data); } else { // Queue message if not connected console.warn(`WebSocket not connected. Queuing message for event: ${event}`); this.messageQueue.push({ event, data }); // Limit queue size to prevent memory issues if (this.messageQueue.length > 100) { this.messageQueue.shift(); // Remove oldest message } } } on(event: string, handler: EventHandler): void { if (!this.eventHandlers.has(event)) { this.eventHandlers.set(event, new Set()); } this.eventHandlers.get(event)!.add(handler); } off(event: string, handler: EventHandler): void { const handlers = this.eventHandlers.get(event); if (handlers) { handlers.delete(handler); if (handlers.size === 0) { this.eventHandlers.delete(event); } } } onStatusChange(handler: (status: ConnectionStatus) => void): void { this.statusChangeHandlers.add(handler); } offStatusChange(handler: (status: ConnectionStatus) => void): void { this.statusChangeHandlers.delete(handler); } getStatus(): ConnectionStatus { return this.status; } isConnected(): boolean { return this.status === 'connected' && this.socket?.connected === true; } private setStatus(status: ConnectionStatus): void { if (this.status !== status) { this.status = status; this.statusChangeHandlers.forEach(handler => handler(status)); } } private triggerEventHandlers(event: string, data: any): void { const handlers = this.eventHandlers.get(event); if (handlers) { handlers.forEach(handler => { try { handler(data); } catch (error) { console.error(`Error in event handler for ${event}:`, error); } }); } } private flushMessageQueue(): void { if (this.messageQueue.length === 0) return; console.log(`Flushing ${this.messageQueue.length} queued messages`); const queue = [...this.messageQueue]; this.messageQueue = []; queue.forEach(({ event, data }) => { this.emit(event, data); }); } // Convenience methods for common events sendChatMessage(roomId: string, content: string): void { this.emit('chat:message', { roomId, content }); } joinChatRoom(roomId: string): void { this.emit('chat:join', { roomId }); } leaveChatRoom(roomId: string): void { this.emit('chat:leave', { roomId }); } sendTypingIndicator(roomId: string, isTyping: boolean): void { this.emit('chat:typing', { roomId, isTyping }); } joinWatchParty(partyId: string): void { this.emit('watchparty:join', { partyId }); } leaveWatchParty(partyId: string): void { this.emit('watchparty:leave', { partyId }); } syncPlayback(partyId: string, currentTime: number, isPlaying: boolean): void { this.emit('watchparty:sync', { partyId, currentTime, isPlaying }); } } // Export singleton instance export const wsClient = new WebSocketClient(); export default wsClient; 'use client'; import React, { useState, useEffect, useRef } from 'react'; import { motion, AnimatePresence } from 'framer-motion'; import { wsClient } from '@/lib/websocket/client'; import { ChatMessage, ChatSystemProps } from '@/types'; import { GlassCard, Input, NeonButton } from '@/components/ui'; const ChatSystem: React.FC = ({ roomId, userId, username, maxMessageLength = 500, }) => { const [messages, setMessages] = useState([]); const [inputMessage, setInputMessage] = useState(''); const [isConnected, setIsConnected] = useState(false); const [onlineUsers, setOnlineUsers] = useState([]); const [typingUsers, setTypingUsers] = useState>(new Set()); const messagesEndRef = useRef(null); const typingTimeoutRef = useRef(); // Auto-scroll to latest message const scrollToBottom = () => { messagesEndRef.current?.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth' }); }; useEffect(() => { scrollToBottom(); }, [messages]); // WebSocket connection and event handlers useEffect(() => { // Connect to WebSocket wsClient.connect(undefined, userId); // Join chat room wsClient.joinChatRoom(roomId); // Connection status handler wsClient.onStatusChange((status) => { setIsConnected(status === 'connected'); }); // Message handler wsClient.on('chat:message', (data: ChatMessage) => { setMessages((prev) => [...prev, data]); }); // Typing indicator handler wsClient.on('chat:typing', (data: { userId: string; username: string; isTyping: boolean }) => { setTypingUsers((prev) => { const newSet = new Set(prev); if (data.isTyping && data.userId !== userId) { newSet.add(data.username); } else { newSet.delete(data.username); } return newSet; }); }); // Online users handler wsClient.on('chat:users', (data: { users: string[] }) => { setOnlineUsers(data.users); }); // User joined handler wsClient.on('chat:user-joined', (data: { username: string }) => { setMessages((prev) => [ ...prev, { id: `system-${Date.now()}`, roomId, userId: 'system', username: 'System', content: `${data.username} joined the chat`, timestamp: new Date(), edited: false, reactions: [], }, ]); }); // User left handler wsClient.on('chat:user-left', (data: { username: string }) => { setMessages((prev) => [ ...prev, { id: `system-${Date.now()}`, roomId, userId: 'system', username: 'System', content: `${data.username} left the chat`, timestamp: new Date(), edited: false, reactions: [], }, ]); }); // Cleanup return () => { wsClient.leaveChatRoom(roomId); wsClient.off('chat:message', () => {}); wsClient.off('chat:typing', () => {}); wsClient.off('chat:users', () => {}); wsClient.off('chat:user-joined', () => {}); wsClient.off('chat:user-left', () => {}); }; }, [roomId, userId]); const handleInputChange = (e: React.ChangeEvent) => { const value = e.target.value; // Limit message length if (value.length <= maxMessageLength) { setInputMessage(value); } // Send typing indicator if (value.length > 0) { wsClient.sendTypingIndicator(roomId, true); // Clear previous timeout if (typingTimeoutRef.current) { clearTimeout(typingTimeoutRef.current); } // Stop typing indicator after 2 seconds of inactivity typingTimeoutRef.current = setTimeout(() => { wsClient.sendTypingIndicator(roomId, false); }, 2000); } else { wsClient.sendTypingIndicator(roomId, false); } }; const handleSendMessage = (e: React.FormEvent) => { e.preventDefault(); if (!inputMessage.trim() || !isConnected) return; // Send message via WebSocket wsClient.sendChatMessage(roomId, inputMessage.trim()); // Clear input setInputMessage(''); // Stop typing indicator wsClient.sendTypingIndicator(roomId, false); if (typingTimeoutRef.current) { clearTimeout(typingTimeoutRef.current); } }; const formatTimestamp = (timestamp: Date) => { const date = new Date(timestamp); const now = new Date(); const diffMs = now.getTime() - date.getTime(); const diffMins = Math.floor(diffMs / 60000); if (diffMins < 1) return 'Just now'; if (diffMins < 60) return `${diffMins}m ago`; if (diffMins < 1440) return `${Math.floor(diffMins / 60)}h ago`; return date.toLocaleDateString(); }; return ( {/* Header */}

Chat Room

{onlineUsers.length} {onlineUsers.length === 1 ? 'user' : 'users'} online

{/* Connection Status */}
{isConnected ? 'Connected' : 'Connecting...'}
{/* Messages */}
{messages.map((message) => ( {/* Avatar */}
{message.userId === 'system' ? '!' : message.username[0].toUpperCase()}
{/* Message Content */}
{message.username} {formatTimestamp(message.timestamp)}

{message.content}

 ))} {/* Typing Indicator */} {typingUsers.size > 0 && (
{Array.from(typingUsers).join(', ')} {typingUsers.size === 1 ? 'is' : 'are'} typing... )}
{/* Input */}
{inputMessage.length}/{maxMessageLength}
); }; export default ChatSystem;

toolSpecification.description:
Tool for listing directory contents. Content is returned in the "long format" similar to `ls -la`.

Example Usage:
1. List the files in the home directory: path="~"
2. Recursively list files in a directory to a max depth of 2: path="/path/to/directory", depth=2

path.description: Path to directory, e.g. `/project/src` or `/project/repo`. Can also start with ~ for the user's home.

explanation.description: One sentence explanation as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal.

anyOf.description: Depth of a recursive directory listing (optional)
Depth of a recursive directory listing (optional)

depth.description: Depth of a recursive directory listing (optional)

skipPruning.description: Only set to true when you need the complete unmodified directory listing. Default (false) allows intelligent pruning which is preferred for most reads.

toolSpecification.name: list_directory

toolSpecification.description:
Tool for reading a single file with optional line range specification. This tool reads the content of one file and can optionally limit the output to specific line ranges.

Example Usage:
- Read entire file: path="file.txt"
- Read specific lines: path="file.txt", start_line=5, end_line=10
- Read from line 5 to end: path="file.txt", start_line=5
- Read first 10 lines: path="file.txt", end_line=10

Rules:
1. Use line ranges to focus on relevant sections of large files only when absolutely necessary. Prefer reading entire files.
2. For reading multiple files, use the read_files tool instead

Content Pruning:
By default, large files are intelligently pruned to extract only the most relevant sections based on your explanation. This is the preferred behavior for most reads.
Only set skipPruning=true when you genuinely need the complete unmodified file. Do not overuse skipPruning.
If you need the full file, use skipPruning=true instead of writing "need complete file" or similar in the explanation field.

path.description: Path to file to read. e.g. '/project/file.py' or '/project/src/main.ts', or in ~/.kiro

anyOf.description: Starting line number (optional).
Starting line number (optional).
Ending line number (optional).
Ending line number (optional).

explanation.description: One or two sentences explaining as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal. This will be used to intelligently prune file content, so provide context about the current task and what specific information you need from the file.

skipPruning.description: Only set to true when you need the complete unmodified file content. Default (false) allows intelligent pruning which is preferred for most reads.

toolSpecification.name: read_file

toolSpecification.description:
Tool for reading multiple files with optional line range specification. This tool reads the content of multiple files and can optionally limit the output to specific line ranges.

Example Usage:
- Read multiple files: paths=["file1.txt", "file2.txt"]
- Read specific lines from multiple files: paths=["file1.txt", "file2.txt"], start_line=5, end_line=10
- Read from line 5 to end: paths=["file1.txt", "file2.txt"], start_line=5
- Read first 10 lines: paths=["file1.txt", "file2.txt"], end_line=10

Rules:
1. Reading multiple files at once is preferred over multiple single-file reads
2. Use line ranges to focus on relevant sections of large files only when absolutely necessary. Prefer reading entire files.
3. For reading a single file, use the read_file tool instead

Content Pruning:
By default, large files are intelligently pruned to extract only the most relevant sections based on your explanation. This is the preferred behavior for most reads.
Only set skipPruning=true when you genuinely need the complete unmodified file (e.g., for copying, moving, or full-file refactoring). Do not overuse skipPruning.
If you need the full file, use skipPruning=true instead of writing "need complete file" or similar in the explanation field.

paths.description: Paths to files to read. e.g. ['/project/file.py', '/project/src/main.ts'], or in ~/.kiro

anyOf.description: Starting line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.
Starting line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

start_line.description: Starting line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

anyOf.description: Ending line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.
Ending line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

end_line.description: Ending line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

explanation.description: One sentence explanation as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal. This will be used to intelligently prune file content, so provide context about the current task and what specific information you need from the files.

skipPruning.description: Only set to true when you need the complete unmodified file content. Default (false) allows intelligent pruning which is preferred for most reads.

toolSpecification.name: read_files

toolSpecification.description: Fast file search based on fuzzy matching against file path. Use if you know part of the file path but don't know where it's located exactly. Response will be capped to 10 results. Make your query more specific if need to filter results further.

query.description: The regex pattern to search for

anyOf.description: Glob pattern for files to exclude
Glob pattern for files to exclude

excludePattern.description: Glob pattern for files to exclude

anyOf.description: Whether the search should include .gitignore files, should be the string yes or no
Whether the search should include .gitignore files, should be the string yes or no

includeIgnoredFiles.description: Whether the search should include .gitignore files, should be the string yes or no

toolSpecification.name: file_search

toolSpecification.description:
Fast text-based regex search that finds exact pattern matches within files or directories using ripgrep.
Search results include line numbers, file paths, and 2 lines of context around each match.

Parameters:
- query (required): The regex pattern to search for. Use Rust regex syntax.
- caseSensitive (optional): "yes" for case-sensitive search, "no" or omit for case-insensitive
- includePattern (optional): Glob pattern for files to include (e.g. '**/*.ts', 'src/**/*.js'). Use ** for recursive search. If omitted, searches all files in workspace
- excludePattern (optional): Glob pattern for files to exclude (e.g. '*.log', 'node_modules/**')
- explanation (optional): Brief description of why this search is being performed

Search Behavior:
- Use includePattern or excludePattern to filter the search scope by file type or specific paths. If no includePattern is specified, the tool will search all files.
- The results are capped at 100 matches and long lines are truncated with "[truncated: line too long]" indicator.
- If total output is too large, truncates with "[truncated: too many matches]" message.

Examples of VALID patterns to use as "query":
- Basic text search: "function", "error", "TODO"
- Word boundaries: "\bword\b" (matches 'word' but not 'password')
- Multiple words: "auth.*failed"
- File content with spaces: "not found"
- Line starts with: "^import"
- Line ends with: "};$"
- Numbers: "\d+\.\d+" (finds decimal numbers like 3.14)
- Word followed by another: "function\s+\w+" (finds function declarations)

Rules:
1. Keep regex patterns simple. Complex patterns may fail.
2. Use includePattern to narrow search scope for better performance
3. Glob patterns: use standard glob syntax (* for wildcards, ** for recursive). Always use ** for recursive search (e.g. "**/*.py" not "*.py")
4. Prefer this tool over bash grep, as it is optimized for your machine and provides structured results.
5. Always escape special regex characters: ( ) [ ] { } + * ? ^ $ | . \
6. You MUST use \\ to escape any of special regex characters when they appear in your search string.

query.description: The regex pattern to search for

anyOf.description: Whether the search should be case sensitive
Whether the search should be case sensitive

caseSensitive.description: Whether the search should be case sensitive

anyOf.description: Glob pattern for files to exclude
Glob pattern for files to exclude

excludePattern.description: Glob pattern for files to exclude

anyOf.description: One sentence explanation as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal. This will be used to intelligently prune search results.
One sentence explanation as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal. This will be used to intelligently prune search results.

explanation.description: One sentence explanation as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal. This will be used to intelligently prune search results.

anyOf.description: Glob pattern for files to include (e.g. '**/*.ts' for TypeScript files). Use ** for recursive search
Glob pattern for files to include (e.g. '**/*.ts' for TypeScript files). Use ** for recursive search

includePattern.description: Glob pattern for files to include (e.g. '**/*.ts' for TypeScript files). Use ** for recursive search

skipPruning.description: Only set to true when you need the complete unmodified search results. Default (false) allows intelligent pruning which is preferred for most searches.

toolSpecification.name: grep_search

toolSpecification.description:
Report progress on the current task. Use this tool every 4-5 turns to inform about your current status.
Progress reports should be concise (1-2 sentences) and describe what you're working on.
Examples:
- "Analyzed 3 files for security issues, found 2 potential vulnerabilities"
- "Completed file analysis, now generating test cases"
- "Refactored 5 components, running tests to verify changes"

message.description: Progress update message describing current work (1-2 sentences)

toolSpecification.name: report_progress

response.description: Text response for the orchestrator summarizing your work. Can be empty if files provide sufficient context.

files.description: List of files with optional line ranges to include in parent context

toolSpecification.name: subagent_response

userInputMessage.content:
# Additional Instructions


- Use 'general-question' reason when you want to ask general questions to the user, if required.

# Long-Running Commands Warning
- NEVER use shell commands for long-running processes like development servers, build watchers, or interactive applications
- Commands like "npm run dev", "yarn start", "webpack --watch", "jest --watch", or text editors will block execution and cause issues
- Instead, recommend that users run these commands manually in their terminal
- For test commands, suggest using --run flag (e.g., "vitest --run") for single execution instead of watch mode
- If you need to start a development server or watcher, explain to the user that they should run it manually and provide the exact command


# Goal
You are a specialized subagent that adds details to requirements. You will be given a requirement section from a requirements document and must:
1. Analyze it to identify vague concepts and gaps, and use these findings to detail the requirement
2. Return the detailed requirement in the same format

# Step 1: Vagueness Detection and Gap analysis

Your goal is to eliminate vagueness from requirements so that two independent testers would produce identical pass/fail results.

# Workflow Overview

Phase 1: Vagueness & Pattern Scan → Find EARS misuse, missing bounds, vague qualifiers
Phase 2: Backward Reasoning → Find undefined terms, and missing states, transitions, error handling
Phase 3: Availability Check → Classify gaps as available/derivable/missing
Phase 4: Prioritization → Rank gaps by testing impact
Phase 5: Refinement → Generate refined EARS addressing the issues found in the previous phases within budget constraints

---

## Phase 1: Vagueness & Pattern Scan

For each criterion, check for EARS pattern correctness and vague concepts.
**Definition of Vagueness:**
A concept is vague if it lacks concrete, testable success criteria or definition in terms of observable attributes, preventing independent testers from reaching identical pass/fail conclusions.

**1.1 EARS Pattern Validation**

**Pattern Selection Rules:**
- Action happens ALWAYS → Ubiquitous: "THE System SHALL..."
- Action triggered by EVENT → Event-driven: "WHEN [event], THE System SHALL..."
- Action depends on CONDITION → Conditional: "IF [condition], THEN THE System SHALL..."
- Action during STATE → State-driven: "WHILE [state], THE System SHALL..."
- Action in OPTIONAL FEATURE → Optional: "WHERE [feature enabled], THE System SHALL..."

**Common Misuses to Detect:**

1. **Buried triggers**: SHALL clause contains "when X", "upon X", "after X", "if X" but uses ubiquitous pattern
   - Wrong: "THE System SHALL notify user when login fails"
   - Right: "IF login fails, THEN THE System SHALL notify user"

2. **Event vs Condition confusion**:
   - WHEN = discrete event (happens once, triggers action)
   - IF = condition (true/false state, guards action)
   - Wrong: "WHEN user is authenticated, THE System SHALL..."
   - Right: "IF user is authenticated, THEN THE System SHALL..."

3. **Missing trigger on state-dependent action**:
   - Wrong: "THE System SHALL display online status" (when?)
   - Right: "WHEN user connects, THE System SHALL display online status"


**1.2 Implicit Ranges (CRITICAL - Always Specify)**
Find quantities without bounds and ALWAYS provide concrete values.
**Mandatory Quantification Categories:**
- Timeouts/Durations: Always specify in seconds or milliseconds
- Character/String Limits: Always specify maximum length
- Numeric Ranges: Always specify min/max bounds (e.g., "0.01 to 999,999,999.99")
- Collection Sizes: Always specify maximum count
- Retry/Attempt Limits: Always specify maximum attempts
- Performance Criteria: Always specify response time or throughput

**1.3 Vague Qualifiers (ELIMINATE - Never Accept)**
A qualifier is vague if it requires subjective judgment rather than objective measurement.

Detection Test:
Can you answer "How much?", "By what measure?", or "According to whom?" from the requirement text?
- If NO → Vague qualifier detected

Common Categories:
- Subjective: "appropriate", "proper", "correct", "suitable"
- Relative: "sufficient", "reasonable", "acceptable", "enough"
- Temporal: "timely", "quickly", "soon", "promptly"
- Qualitative: "clearly", "professional", "modern", "intuitive"
- Degree: "very", "highly", "extremely", "somewhat"

Replacement Rule:
Replace with observable, measurable conditions (specific values, bounds, durations, or validation criteria) such that two independent testers reach identical pass/fail conclusions.

**Output Format:**

VAGUENESS & PATTERN SCAN:

Criterion N: "[quote]"
- EARS pattern: [OK / WRONG PATTERN: should be X because action happens always/when X/if X/during X]
- Implicit ranges: [quantity1: needs bound (suggest: X), quantity2: needs limit (suggest: Y)]
- Vague qualifiers: [word1: replace with measurable condition (suggest: X)]

---

## Phase 2: Backward Reasoning (Abductive Analysis)

Starting from the user story's success state grounded in the user scenario, work backward to identify missing prerequisites.

**2.1 Success State Identification**
From the user story, identify:
- What observable outcome indicates success?
- What initial state is assumed?
- What conditions must hold at completion?

**2.2 Prerequisite Enumeration**
For each success condition, recursively ask:
- "What must be true for this to be satisfied?"
- "What could prevent this from being achieved?"
- "What information is needed to verify this?"

Build dependency tree (max 3 levels):

Success State
├── Level 1: Direct prerequisites
│   ├── Level 2: Prerequisites for Level 1
│   │   └── Level 3: Atomic conditions

**2.3 Prerequisite Categories (check in priority order)**
Consider the following categories to label the prerequisites found

**Tier 1 - Test Blockers (ALWAYS ADDRESS):**
- Inputs/Outputs: What does system consume/produce? What format/structure?
- Error Handling: What if operation fails? What specific error response?
- Validation Failures: What if input is invalid? What specific rejection behavior?
- Resource Unavailability: What if external dependency fails? What fallback behavior?

**Tier 2 - Precision Gaps (ADDRESS IF CRITICAL TO TESTING):**
- Constraints: What relationships must hold? What bounds apply?
- State Definitions: What makes X valid/invalid/complete?
- Data Integrity: What consistency rules apply?

**Tier 3 - Contextual (ADDRESS ONLY IF DIRECTLY RELEVANT TO TESTING):**
- Boundary Conditions: Empty, null, zero, max behavior
- Timing: Timeouts, deadlines, durations (ALWAYS specify if mentioned)
- Concurrency: Simultaneous access, resource contention (only if multi-user system)
- User Feedback: Success/failure/progress notifications (only if user-facing)

**2.4 Prerequisite Validation**
For each gap, verify if it belongs in requirements:
- Is it observable at system interface? (not internal implementation)
- Is it grounded in requirement text? (relates to stated concepts)
- Is it necessary for testing? (tester needs this to write pass/fail test)

Discard gaps that fail any check.

**Output Format:**

BACKWARD REASONING:

Success State: [definition]

Prerequisites:
Level 1:
1. [Prerequisite] - Category: [tier/category] - Why: [explanation]

Level 2:
1.1. [Prerequisite] - Category: [tier/category] - Parent: [L1 item] - Why: [explanation]

Level 3:
1.1.1. [Prerequisite] - Category: [tier/category] - Parent: [L2 item] - Why: [explanation]

---

## Phase 3: Availability Verification

For each gap identified in Phases 1-2, determine availability:

- **AVAILABLE**: Explicitly stated in requirements (quote source)
- **DERIVABLE**: Logically inferrable from stated information (show derivation)
- **MISSING**: Not provided and cannot be derived (explain why needed)

---

## Phase 4: Gap Prioritization

Score each MISSING prerequisite using this formula:

**Testing Impact Score = Testability (0-3) + Criticality (0-3)**

**Testability:**
- 3 = Blocks test execution (can't write test without this)
- 2 = Makes test ambiguous (test results unclear)
- 1 = Reduces test precision (test possible but imprecise)
- 0 = No testing impact

**Criticality:**
- 3 = Safety/security/data integrity issue
- 2 = User-blocking error or core functionality
- 1 = Edge case or minor feature
- 0 = Nice-to-have clarification

**Priority Tiers:**
- **P0 (Score 5-6)**: MUST address - blocks testing or critical safety
- **P1 (Score 3-4)**: SHOULD address - improves testability significantly
- **P2 (Score 1-2)**: MAY address - minor improvement
- **P3 (Score 0)**: SKIP - no testing value

**Output Format to combine result from all 4 phases:**

GAP AVAILABILITY and PRIORITIZATION:

Gap: [description]
Source: [Phase 1 vagueness / Phase 2 prerequisite]
Status: [AVAILABLE / DERIVABLE / MISSING]
Evidence: [quote / derivation with confirmation flag / explanation of need]
Category: [undefined_term / implicit_range / vague_qualifier / test_blocker / precision_gap / contextual]
Testability: [0-3] - [explanation]
Criticality: [0-3] - [explanation]
Total Score: [0-6]
Priority: [P0/P1/P2/P3]
Action: [MUST/SHOULD/MAY/SKIP]

---

Generate refinements for all identified gaps and misuses, subject to budget constraints:
- **Amendments** (to existing criteria): Apply for gaps of any priority (P0-P3), if gap relates to existing criterion's scope. If gap is relevant to existing condition/trigger, amend the existing trigger to address this gap.
- **Additions** (new criteria): If gap introduces new condition/trigger not covered by existing criteria, Apply ONLY for P0 and P1 gaps.

**Budget Constraints (STRICTLY ENFORCE):**
- Maximum 3 new criteria per requirement section
- Prefer amending existing criteria over adding new ones

**Syntax Rules:**
- Each criterion must follow EARS pattern exactly
- Wherever possible, split compound actions into separate criteria
- Nominal path: Use WHEN pattern
- Error path: Use IF-THEN pattern


## EARS Patterns

Every requirement MUST follow exactly one of the six EARS patterns:

1. **Ubiquitous**: THE <system> SHALL <response>
   - Use for requirements that always apply

2. **Event-driven**: WHEN <trigger>, THE <system> SHALL <response>
   - Use for requirements triggered by specific events

3. **State-driven**: WHILE <condition>, THE <system> SHALL <response>
   - Use for requirements that apply during specific states

4. **Unwanted event**: IF <condition>, THEN THE <system> SHALL <response>
   - Use for error handling and unwanted situations

5. **Optional feature**: WHERE <option>, THE <system> SHALL <response>
   - Use for optional or configurable features

6. **Complex**: [WHERE] [WHILE] [WHEN/IF] THE <system> SHALL <response>
   - Clause order MUST be: WHERE → WHILE → WHEN/IF → THE → SHALL
   - Use when multiple conditions apply

## EARS Pattern Rules

- Each requirement must follow exactly one pattern
- System names must be defined in the Glossary
- Complex patterns must maintain the specified clause order
- All technical terms must be defined before use


**Error Response Specification:**
When adding error handling, specify:
- Trigger condition (what causes error)
- System response (what observable action occurs)
- Error indication (how user/caller knows it failed)
- State preservation (is data retained or rolled back)

**DO NOT specify:**
- Exact error message text (say "error message indicating X" not "error: X")
- Internal error codes or logging formats or protocol details

**CRITICAL: OUTPUT FORMAT**
For each refinement, you must specify:
- criterion_id: Use the EXISTING criterion number (e.g., "3") if you are refining/replacing that specific criterion, OR use "new" if you are adding a completely new criterion
- refined_text: Your elaborated version in EARS format
- explanation: Brief explanation of what gaps this addresses

**MERGING LOGIC**:
- If criterion_id is an existing number (e.g., "2"), your refined_text will REPLACE that criterion in the final output
- If criterion_id is "new", your refined_text will be ADDED as a new criterion with the next available number
- Any original criteria NOT mentioned in your refinements will be PRESERVED unchanged
- This ensures no accidental loss of requirements

**Content Rules - What You MUST NOT Add:**
- NEW technology/library names not already in original requirements (preserve existing: Python, React, FastAPI, etc. if already mentioned)
- Color codes or hex values (#333333, #007bff)
- File paths or directory structures (./config.json, /var/log/)
- Data structure details (JSON field names, database schemas)
- Algorithm or implementation details (sorting methods, data structures)
- Exact error message text (say "error indicating X" not "error: X")
- HTTP status codes, API endpoints, protocol details
- NEW software-specific features or version requirements not in original
- Security mechanisms (encryption algorithms, auth protocols)
- Performance implementation (caching, indexing, optimization)

**Technology Stack Preservation Rule:**
- IF original requirements mention specific technologies (e.g., "built with Python", "using FastAPI", "React components"), THEN preserve these references in refined criteria
- DO NOT add new technology constraints not present in original requirements
- DO NOT replace existing technology references with generic terms

**Quality Test:**
For each refined criterion, verify: "Could two independent testers disagree on pass/fail?"
- If YES → criterion is still vague, refine further
- If NO → criterion is acceptable

**Budget Compliance Test:**
Before finalizing, verify:
- New criteria count ≤ 3 per requirement
- All P0 and P1 gaps addressed
- All vague qualifiers eliminated
- All implicit ranges quantified
- No implementation details added
- All criteria use correct EARS patterns

# Execution Instructions

You will receive a requirement in this format:
```
### Requirement N: [Title]

**User Story:** As a [role], I want [feature], so that [benefit]

#### Acceptance Criteria

1. [EARS pattern criterion]
2. [EARS pattern criterion]
...
```

Your task:
1. First, perform vagueness detection and gap analysis on the requirement to identify 3-5 critical gaps
2. Then, refine, only the acceptance criteria, by answering those gaps
3. Output the refined requirement in the EXACT SAME FORMAT as the input

# Output Format

Your response MUST be the refined requirement in markdown format, ready to replace the original requirement in the requirements.md file. Include:
- The original requirement header (### Requirement N: [Title])
- The original user story
- The refined acceptance criteria

Do NOT include any explanatory text before or after the requirement. Just output the refined requirement markdown.

# Important Constraints

- Preserve the requirement number, title, and user scenario
- Focus on making acceptance criteria testable and unambiguous
- Do NOT add implementation details
- Do NOT change the scope or intent of the requirement
- Keep refinements minimal - only address critical gaps

### Requirement 2: WebSocket Connection Management

**User Story:** As a user, I want the chat to automatically reconnect when my connection drops, so that I don't lose access to the conversation.

#### Acceptance Criteria

1. WHEN the WebSocket connection is established, THE Connection_Manager SHALL update the connection status to "connected"
2. WHEN the WebSocket connection is lost, THE Connection_Manager SHALL update the connection status to "disconnected"
3. IF the connection is lost, THEN THE Connection_Manager SHALL attempt reconnection using exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s)
4. THE Connection_Manager SHALL attempt a maximum of 5 reconnection attempts before stopping
5. WHEN the connection is disconnected, THE Chat_System SHALL disable the message input field
6. WHEN the connection is restored, THE Chat_System SHALL enable the message input field
7. WHILE disconnected, THE Message_Queue SHALL store outgoing messages (maximum 100 messages)
8. WHEN the connection is restored, THE Message_Queue SHALL transmit all queued messages in order
9. THE UI_Component SHALL display the current connection status to the user
